note: Chaloner & Verdinelli, 'Bayesian Experimental Design: A Review', Statistical Science 10(3):273-304, 1995 (IMS; JSTOR 2246015). BLIND full read via OCR (JSTOR scan: pdftotext yields only the cover page, so the 33-page body was rendered at 300 DPI and tesseract-OCR'd). This IS the classical BED review. It presents the normative decision-theoretic design criterion = MAXIMIZE EXPECTED UTILITY: Eq. (1) U(eta)=int int U(d,theta,eta,y) p(theta|y,eta) p(y|eta) d theta dy, Eq. (2) 'The Bayesian solution to the experimental design problem is provided by the design eta* maximizing (1)' (p.275, after Raiffa & Schlaifer 1961 and Lindley 1972 pp.19-20). Expected Information Gain is explicitly the canonical information utility: p.277 'several authors (Stone 1959; DeGroot 1962,1986; Bernardo 1979) considered the expected gain in Shannon information ... proposed choosing a design that maximizes the expected gain in Shannon information or, equivalently, maximizes the expected Kullback-Leibler distance between the posterior and the prior distributions' (Eq. 3), with Eq. (4) U1(eta)=int int log{p(theta|y,eta)} p(y,theta|eta) d theta dy the 'expected Shannon information of the posterior', following Lindley 1956 / Shannon 1948; and 'a utility function based on Shannon information leads to Bayesian D-optimality' (p.275, Bernardo 1979). This 'expected gain in Shannon information = expected KL(posterior||prior)' is exactly the manuscript's EIG = E[H(theta)-H(theta|y,d)]. Two manuscript claims checked. C036 (L78, EIG normative criterion) = ACCURATE. C037 (L94, 'classical, with a long history') = ACCURATE -- within the {chaloner1995,rainforth2024} pair, chaloner1995 is precisely the CLASSICAL anchor (the 1995 Statistical Science review that Rainforth 2024 explicitly supersedes), and it traces BED's lineage to Lindley 1956/1972, Raiffa & Schlaifer 1961, Stone 1959, DeGroot 1962, Bernardo 1979. Boundary worth noting (advisory, non-blocking): the paper's GENERAL normative criterion is expected-utility maximization, of which EIG is the information-based special case; the bulk treats 'alphabetical' criteria (A/c/D/E/G), and Bayes E- and G-optimality are explicitly NOT utility-based. The paper predates all modern EIG computation (nested/multilevel MC, variational bounds, amortized DAD/iDAD, RL) -- those belong to rainforth2024modern, the modern co-citation.

C036.claim_text: Bayesian Experimental Design~\citep{chaloner1995bayesian,rainforth2024modern} provides a normative criterion for the information objective of the search policy $\pi$: select $d^*$ maximizing Expected Information Gain (EIG), $d^* = \arg\max_{d} \mathrm{EIG}(d) = \arg\max_{d} \mathbb{E}[H(\theta) - H(\theta \mid y, d)]$.
evidence: (a) chaloner1995 IS the classical BED review and DOES supply the normative criterion -- CONFIRMED. Abstract (p.273): 'This paper reviews the literature on Bayesian experimental design. A unified view of this topic is presented, based on a decision-theoretic approach.' The normative rule is stated at p.275: Eq. (1) U(eta) = int int U(d,theta,eta,y) p(theta|y,eta) p(y|eta) d theta dy is the expected utility of the best decision for design eta; Eq. (2): 'The Bayesian solution to the experimental design problem is provided by the design eta* maximizing (1)'; and in words: 'a good way to design experiments is to specify a utility function reflecting the purpose of the experiment, to regard the design choice as a decision problem and to select a design that maximizes the expected utility.' => 'provides a normative criterion ... select d* maximizing [expected utility]'.

(b) The EIG formula in the manuscript is exactly chaloner's information utility. p.277 (Section 2.2): 'several authors (Stone, 1959a,b; DeGroot, 1962, 1986; Bernardo, 1979) considered the expected gain in Shannon information given by an experiment as a utility function (Shannon, 1948). These authors proposed choosing a design that maximizes the expected gain in Shannon information or, equivalently, maximizes the expected Kullback-Leibler distance between the posterior and the prior distributions' -- Eq. (3): int int log[p(theta|y,eta)/p(theta)] p(y,theta|eta) d theta dy. The 'expected gain in Shannon information = expected KL(posterior||prior)' is definitionally EIG = E[H(theta) - H(theta|y,d)] = mutual information I(theta;y|d). Eq. (4) U1(eta)=int int log{p(theta|y,eta)} p(y,theta|eta) d theta dy is named 'the expected Shannon information of the posterior distribution', used 'when the experiment is conducted for inference on the vector theta'. Design 'd' (manuscript) = design 'eta' (paper); target theta and outcome y identical.

(c) Explicit info-utility <-> criterion link. p.275: 'a utility function based on Shannon information leads to Bayesian D-optimality in the normal linear model (see Bernardo, 1979).' Traces to Lindley (1956) 'suggestion' (p.277) -- i.e. this paper documents the classical origin of the EIG design criterion the manuscript invokes.

(d) Precision note (does not change verdict): chaloner's GENERAL normative criterion is expected-UTILITY maximization (Eqs 1-2), and EIG/Shannon-information gain is its canonical information-based instance (Eqs 3-4); the paper also develops non-information utilities (A/c-optimality from squared-error loss) and criteria that are NOT utility-based (Bayes E-, G-optimality, p.278). The manuscript scopes its citation to 'the information objective ... maximizing EIG', which is squarely the Eq.(3)/(4) content, so the attribution is correct. The co-citation rainforth2024modern (separately verified ACCURATE, its C038) carries the explicit modern 'EIG' vocabulary and estimation machinery.

C037.claim_text: The POMDP framework~\citep{kaelbling1998pomdp} and BED~\citep{chaloner1995bayesian,rainforth2024modern} are classical, with a long history of Bayesian-optimal experiment selection in automated science---most directly the Robot Scientist's active-learning policy~\citep{sparkes2010robot}; we connect POMDP (structure) to BED (normative policy) for the LLM-agent setting rather than claiming priority for the idea.
evidence: The descriptor 'classical, with a long history' applies to BED-the-field, and chaloner1995 is the paradigm CLASSICAL anchor for it: a 1995 Statistical Science REVIEW whose whole purpose is to consolidate two-plus decades of BED. p.273: 'Bayesian experimental design has gained popularity in the past two decades' (i.e. since ~1975, as of 1995).

It documents the long lineage the manuscript invokes: Lindley (1972, pp.19-20) and Raiffa & Schlaifer (1961) for the decision-theoretic formulation (p.275); Lindley (1956), Stone (1959a,b), DeGroot (1962, 1986), Bernardo (1979), Shannon (1948) for the information-gain utility (p.277); Chernoff (1953) for local optimality (p.287). This is precisely 'a long history of Bayesian-optimal experiment selection.'

Within the co-cited pair, the 'classical' weight rests correctly on chaloner1995: the modern companion rainforth2024modern (title 'Modern Bayesian Experimental Design') explicitly positions itself as superseding 'previous reviews', namely Chaloner & Verdinelli 1995 [23] and Ryan et al. 2016 [136]. So calling chaloner1995 'classical' is exact, not loose. (The sibling adjudication marked rainforth2024's version of this sentence IMPRECISE-OK for the opposite reason -- rainforth is NOT itself classical; chaloner absorbs that 'classical' weight cleanly.)

No misattribution: chaloner is credited only with being a classical BED reference of long standing, which it is. The sentence's hedge 'rather than claiming priority for the idea' is consistent with the paper's own framing that BED builds on decades of prior work.

reverse_findings.fact: chaloner1995's GENERAL normative criterion is MAXIMIZE EXPECTED UTILITY (Eqs 1-2, p.275); Expected Information Gain (expected gain in Shannon information = expected posterior-to-prior KL, Eqs 3-4, p.277) is the canonical INFORMATION utility, i.e. one special case. The paper devotes most of its length to 'alphabetical' criteria (A/c/D/E/G-optimality), and it explicitly notes that Bayes E-optimality and G-optimality 'do not correspond to maximizing a utility function' and their decision-theoretic justification is 'unclear' (p.278).
reverse_findings.loc: p.275 (Eqs 1-2); p.277 (Eqs 3-4, Shannon-info/EIG); p.278 (E-/G-optimality not utility-based)
reverse_findings.mismatch: None that blocks C036/C037. Advisory: chaloner supports EIG as THE information objective (Eqs 3-4) and expected-utility as the OVERARCHING normative criterion; any manuscript passage that presented chaloner1995 as an exclusively-EIG or purely information-theoretic reference would slightly narrow it. As written, C036 scopes to 'the information objective ... EIG', which is accurate.

reverse_findings.fact: chaloner1995 predates ALL modern EIG computation. It obtains designs analytically, by numerical optimization, by normal approximations to the posterior + expected Fisher information, and by local optimality (Chernoff 1953). It contains NO nested/multi-level Monte Carlo EIG estimators, NO variational EIG bounds, NO amortized/policy-based design (DAD/iDAD), and NO reinforcement learning. It never uses the term 'EIG', 'mutual information', or 'policy' -- its vocabulary is 'expected gain in Shannon information' / 'expected utility'.
reverse_findings.loc: Sections 4.2-4.4 (approximations, local optimality); Section 5.4 (software: Clyde 1993 XLISP-STAT, Chaloner & Larntz 1988 FORTRAN)
reverse_findings.mismatch: None with C036/C037. Relevant to the WHOLE manuscript's BED narrative: the modern estimation/optimization machinery (variational, amortized, RL) belongs to the co-citation rainforth2024modern, NOT chaloner1995. Any sentence attributing modern EIG estimators or amortized/RL policies to chaloner1995 alone would be a misattribution -- keep those on rainforth2024modern / foster2019variational / foster2021dad / blau2022rl.

reverse_findings.fact: A signature qualitative result of BED per chaloner1995: for NONLINEAR models the number of support points in an optimal Bayesian design increases as the prior distribution becomes more dispersed (Chaloner & Larntz 1986, 1989), enabling model checking; local (point-prior) optimality is a 'crude approximation' to expected utility. For LINEAR models with sigma^2 known, Bayesian and non-Bayesian designs coincide as n grows or the prior becomes diffuse -- 'when a noninformative prior distribution is used ... there is no advantage to using the Bayesian approach for design' (p.279).
reverse_findings.loc: p.289 (support points vs prior dispersion); p.279 (linear large-n / diffuse-prior collapse); p.287 (local optimality as crude approx)
reverse_findings.mismatch: None with C036/C037. Advisory context for other manuscript claims about approximate/uncertainty-based acquisition: chaloner frames local optimality and normal/Fisher-information approximations as APPROXIMATIONS to the exact expected-utility (EIG) ideal, which supports characterizing heuristic uncertainty-sampling schemes as 'approximating' BED but not as exact EIG maximization.
